coredata.dc:title: A novel cationic surfactant synthesized from carbon quantum dots and the versatility
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuan
Wu, Peng
Lei, Chen
Liu, Xiang
Han, Xinya
coredata.dc:description: An approach of manufacturing a novel cationic surfactant derived from carbon quantum dots with versatility of fluorescence, surface activity and antibacterial performance was described in detail in this work. Firstly, hydrothermal treatment was carried out on mixture of citric acid, N,N-dimethylethylenediamine and hydrogen peroxide for obtaining the carbon quantum dots (represented with OX-CQDs). Successively, quaternization of OX-CQDs with 1-chlorododecane was executed in ethanol medium for preparing the novel cationic surfactant (represented with OX-CQDs-C12H25). The measurement showed that the fluorescent quantum yield of OX-CQDs-C12H25 approached 23.13%. Surface tension of the dilute solution reached a minimum of 30.0 mN m−1, which was comparable to some cationic Gemini surfactants. Additionally, it performed synergistic surface effect when OX-CQDs-C12H25 was mixed with sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate. Also, OX-CQDs-C12H25 still performed well in inhibiting growth of Escherichia coli when the solution was diluted to 0.21 mg mL−1. Such performances of OX-CQDs-C12H25 were superior to that of the cationic surfactant obtained by similar way without hydrogen peroxide in surface activity, fluorescent quantum yield and antibiosis for Escherichia coli.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Quaternization
Cationic surfactant
Surface tension
Versatility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analyses on IR and 1H NMR spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25
PL and UV–vis spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25
Theoretic analyses on surface absorption capacity of OX-CQDs-C12H25 solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic description of preparing the novel cationic CQDs surfactant.
Images of transmission electron microscopy of OX-CQDs and the distribution of particle size.
IR and 1H NMR spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 corresponding to (a) and (b) respectively. Inset of (b) is enlarged range between 2.0 and 0.5 ppm.

(a, b) PL spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 varied with excitation of 340, 360 and 380 nm; (c) UV–vis spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25; (d) Peak areas of the PL spectra of the dilute solutions varied with the absorbances. Insets of (a) and (b) show the bright blue fluorescence of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 respectively.

(a) Surface tensions of dilute OX-CQDs-C12H25 solutions varied with the concentrations. The curve was obtained by nonlinear fitting for obtaining a math function that expresses relationship between surface tensions and the concentrations; (b) Plots of surface absorption capacities of OX-CQDs-C12H25 solutions versus the concentrations.
Contact angles of OX-CQDs-C12H25 solutions varied with the concentrations.
Antibacterial percentage of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 varied with the concentrations.
Surface tensions of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate solutions (10 mg mL−1) varied with additional amounts of OX-CQDs-C12H25 (25 °C).

row.entry: Surface tension of the mixed solutions (mN m−1)

head.ce:title: A novel cationic surfactant synthesized from carbon quantum dots and the versatility

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An approach of manufacturing a novel cationic surfactant derived from carbon quantum dots with versatility of fluorescence, surface activity and antibacterial performance was described in detail in this work. Firstly, hydrothermal treatment was carried out on mixture of citric acid, N,N-dimethylethylenediamine and hydrogen peroxide for obtaining the carbon quantum dots (represented with OX-CQDs). Successively, quaternization of OX-CQDs with 1-chlorododecane was executed in ethanol medium for preparing the novel cationic surfactant (represented with OX-CQDs-C12H25). The measurement showed that the fluorescent quantum yield of OX-CQDs-C12H25 approached 23.13%. Surface tension of the dilute solution reached a minimum of 30.0 mN m−1, which was comparable to some cationic Gemini surfactants. Additionally, it performed synergistic surface effect when OX-CQDs-C12H25 was mixed with sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate. Also, OX-CQDs-C12H25 still performed well in inhibiting growth of Escherichia coli when the solution was diluted to 0.21 mg mL−1. Such performances of OX-CQDs-C12H25 were superior to that of the cationic surfactant obtained by similar way without hydrogen peroxide in surface activity, fluorescent quantum yield and antibiosis for Escherichia coli.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) captured extensive interests in recent years due to the wonderful performance in photoluminescence (PL), cheap raw materials, convenient approach of preparation and low cytotoxicity. CQDs were always obtained by one-pot synthesis with small molecules or carbohydrates under violent conditions. That induced complicate reactions among the raw materials accompanied by the generation of various functional groups on surface of as-prepared CQDs. Such functional groups provided favorable interface for the direct applications of CQDs. For instance, CQDs derived from urea and p-phenylenediamine [1] could disperse in the cytoplasm predominantly, which demonstrated by image of fluorescence confocal microscopy. The hydrophilicity of the CQDs should be due to the dense surface amino groups. CQDs made from carbonization of chitosan [2] possessed well water solubility that was also due to the surface hydroxyl and amino groups. They could penetrate into A549 cells judged by photoluminescent spots in the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area. Carboxylic acid-enriched CQDs could be served as suitable basis of catching nucleic acid which was detected by fluorescent quenching [3]. The CQDs with well water solubility could be anchored by nano ZnO particles, which sensitized them for absorption of visible light. That induced effective charge isolation and brought about an enhancement of photo-conversion efficiency [4]. The rich surface functional groups provided well hydrophilicity that is decisive for the applications. The hydrophilicity of CQDs was up to hydrophilic groups of the raw materials. The resources with carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide or amino groups leaded to well water solubility of CQDs [5]. In fact, the type of surface functional groups was vital for performance of CQDs. For example, CQDs derived from citric acid and imidazolium-based ionic liquid were positive charged due to the quaternary ammonium. Thus phase transfer occurred spontaneously between water and oil phase [6] due to the amphiphilicity of the CQDs solution. From the view of this point, appropriate resources are decisive for synthesizing the CQDs with specific functions.

As a matter of fact, the surface functional groups of CQDs could be tuned via chemical decorations. That enlarged applications of CQDs. Numerous organic compounds with specific functional groups could be served as candidates of the chemical modification. Obviously, an appropriate decorator should be deliberated carefully. For example, PEGylation on carbon dots induced luminescence [7]. Diamine-terminated oligomeric poly-(ethylene glycol) combined with surface carboxyl leaded to bright fluorescence. Branched poly(ethylenimine)-passivated carbon nano dots with positive charges could act with DNA via electrostatic interactions that realized gene transfection [8]. Surface hydroxyl of CQDs could be substituted by chlorine with thionyl chloride and realized the reaction with amino groups of bis(3-aminopropyl)-terminated oligomeric poly(ethylene glycol) [9]. Such modified CQDs were mostly resided in cell membrane and cytoplasm and without any meaningful presence in the cell nucleus. Citric acid was often chosen as a resource for preparing CQDs. However, doping of polyethyleneimine enhanced fluorescent quantum yield dramatically [8,10]. CQDs derived from citric acid were enriched with carboxyl groups [6,10–13], which could be activated by N-hydroxysuccinimide [14–16] catalyzed by N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride or dicyclohexylcarbodiimide. Thus biomolecules containing primary amino groups could be attached covalently onto the CQDs for realizing the fluorescent labeling. Dendrimer of polyamidoamine could be attached covalently with CQDs utilizing the similar approach for selective detection of breast cancer and gene therapy [17]. From the view of these examples, type of the surface functional groups of CQDs was mainly determined by the raw materials. CQDs with special functions require elaborate designing of surface functional groups and proper successive chemical modifications.

In this work, citric acid and tertiary amine were chosen as raw materials for preparing the CQDs intentionally. The carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups ensured the hydrophilicity of the CQDs. The tertiary amino group provided probability of quaternization by chloric hydrocarbon. The introduced long carbon chains of chloric hydrocarbon endowed the hydrophobicity while the inherent surface functional groups provide well hydrophilicity. Such an amphiphilicity predicted surface activity of the as-modified CQDs, which belonged to a cationic surfactant. The process was depicted schematically in Fig. 1.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.5%), N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (99%), hydrogen peroxide (30% in water), 1-chlorododecane (98%), quinine sulfate dehydrate (99.0%) and sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (technical grade) were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). Double distilled water was used throughout the work.

Weighed 3.0 g citric acid monohydrate (0.014 mol) and dissolved in 5 mL water. The solution was mixed with 3.92 mL N,N-dimethylethylenediamine (0.042 mol) and 0.5 mL hydrogen peroxide (30% in water). The amine is excess that ensured alkalinity of the mixture at the end of the reaction. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and was heated at 180 °C for 1 h. When the reactor was cooled to ambient temperature the produced solution (pH = 8.9) was dumped into a round flask. Finally, reduced pressure distillation was carried out for removing the excess amine and solvent water. OX-CQDs were obtained by drying the solution at 80 °C in an electrical oven.

Similar experiments were executed with raw materials of citric acid and N,N-dimethylethylenediamine without hydrogen peroxide. The product was denoted with CQDs. Both two quantum dots (CQDs and OX-CQDs) were chosen as precursors of preparing the cationic CQDs surfactant.

Weighed 5.0 g OX-CQDs and dissolved in 15 mL ethanol completely. And 4 mL (0.014 mol) 1-chlorododecane was mixed with the solution. The mixture was agitated for 5 h with a reflux heating of 70 °C. Then the solvent ethanol was removed by reduced pressure distillation. Successively, 10 mL water was added in for dissolving quaternized OX-CQDs. The aqueous solution was isolated and was dried entirely at 70 °C for obtaining the quaternized OX-CQDs which represented with OX-CQDs-C12H25. The whole process was described schematically in Fig. 1.
Quaternization on CQDs with 1-chlorododecane was also carried out with the similar approach. The product represented with CQDs-C12H25.

OX-CQDs-C12H25 or CQDs-C12H25 with hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups should be amphipathic in aqueous medium as described in Fig. 1. Maximum bubble pressure method was employed to measure surface tensions of OX-CQDs-C12H25 solutions of 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 mg mL−1 at a constant temperature of 25 °C. Surface tensions of the solution of sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (10 mg mL−1) as well as the solution mixed with OX-CQDs-C12H25 were also measured for investigating if there was a synergistic effect between the anionic surfactant of sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate and the as-prepared cationic surfactant of OX-CQDs-C12H25.

Various concentrations of OX-CQDs-C12H25 in test bacteria solution (Escherichia coli TOP10) were prepared firstly. Then the solutions as well as a sample of positive control were added in a 96-wells plate. It was placed in a biochemical incubator with a constant temperature of 37 °C for an incubation of 18 h. Finally, the optical density values were estimated with an enzymatic marker. Bacterial growth rates were determined by measuring the optical density at 600 nm. Thus the antibacterial rates were obtained via calculations on these optical density values.

OX-CQDs were demonstrated by images of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company). The quaternization of OX-CQDs with 1-chlorododecane was explained by enhancement of atomic percentage of C element, which analyzed from X-ray photoelectron energy spectrum (KRATOS AMICUS). Such modification was also proved by spectra of 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) (BRUKER 400, TMS as an internal standard). PL and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 with varied excitations were measured (LS 45, PerkinElmer and P9, MAPADA) for obtaining the fluorescent emissions and absorptions. Fluorescent quantum yields (QY) of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 were calculated by comparison with a standard substance of quinine sulfate in 0.05 mol L−1 sulfuric acid (the fluorescence QY = 55.0%), as was depicted in detail in our previous report [18,19].

For the purpose of preparing the novel cationic CQDs surfactant, N,N-dimethylethylenediamine are chosen intentionally as a raw material. Tertiary amino groups are introduced into the hydrophilic CQDs accordingly. The primary amino group of N,N-dimethylethylenediamine ensures the combination with citric acid. Additionally, the solubility of citric acid guarantees the hydrophilicity of produced CQDs. The added hydrogen peroxide, as an oxidant, contributes to produce oxygen-contained functional groups that are advantageous for hydrophilicity of OX-CQDs. It is also contributable for the enhancement of fluorescent quantum yield according to our experiences [19]. Considering the purity of product, the amount of N,N-dimethylethylenediamine was excess slightly that ensures complete conversion of citric acid. Excess citric acid is hard to be removed. They produce the excess protons, which will combine with tertiary amino groups that prohibit the successive quaternization. The unconsumed N,N-dimethylethylenediamine can be recycled since it is easily removed by distillation in vacuum. Thus the excess amines favor not only the complete conversion of citric acid but also the successive quaternization.

Images of transmission electron microscopy shown in Fig. 2 demonstrate as-prepared OX-CQDs. The sizes are about 1.5 nm with the lattice distance of 0.2 nm, which is approximate with the reported CQDs [1,20–22]. Fig. S1 of Supporting Information (SI) also proves the production of CQDs prepared without hydrogen peroxide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses on IR and 1H NMR spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25
ce:section.ce:para: IR spectrum of OX-CQDs is exhibited in Fig. 3(a). Wide absorption between 3500 and 3250 cm−1 should be assigned to stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H. Bands of 1666 and 1570 cm−1 are due to amide I and II, which predicts the combination between carboxyl of citric acid and primary amino of N,N-dimethylethylenediamine. Peak at 1040 cm−1 belongs to characteristic absorption of ether. The weak peak at 1768 cm−1 should be arisen from stretching vibrations of CO. All those functional groups contribute the hydrophilicity of OX-CQDs. Additionally, the faint peak at 3059 cm−1 should be due to stretching vibrations of H–C of vinyl. Such signals exclaim the unsaturated species in OX-CQDs. The faint quadruple peaks near 3000 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration of C–H of –CH2 or –CH3.

Compared with IR spectrum of OX-CQDs, OX-CQDs-C12H25 does not provide distinct signals of the aliphatic hydrocarbon chain. It can not provide reasonable evidences of demonstrating the quaternization. However, it declares that the quaternization of OX-CQDs with 1-chlorododecane does not arouse any changes of the hydrophilicity.

1H NMR spectrum supplies a clear evidence of proving the quaternization, as is displayed in Fig. 3(b) (chemical shift of 4.7 ppm is due to the solvent D2O). Chemical shift of 1.07–1.16 ppm of OX-CQDs-C12H25, shown in the inset of Fig. 3, is arisen from –CH2CH 2– of alkyl [23,24], which supplies the evidence that the –C12H25 are attached to OX-CQDs. The terminal –CH 3 of such alkyl is witnessed by chemical shift of 0.65–0.71 ppm. The signals near 1.80 ppm exhibited in the inset should be due to –CH 2–CH=CH–. Chemical shifts of 2.38–2.48, 2.73–2.93, 3.10–3.46 ppm should be belonged to –CH 2–NH2, –CH2–CH 2–(CO)– and –CH 2–O–CH2– respectively. Methylene groups with different chemical environments produce several modes of asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibrations, which is consist with IR analyses on Fig. 3(a).

X-ray photoelectron energy spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25, shown in Fig. S2(a) of SI, also prove the quaternization. Atomic percentages of C, N and O of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 are summarized in Table S1 of SI, which shows an enhanced percentage of elemental C. Such enhanced fractions are assigned to the quaternization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL and UV–vis spectra of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25
ce:section.ce:para: Florescent spectra of OX-CQDs are exhibited in Fig. 4(a). OX-CQDs emit bright blue light when irradiated by UV light with wavelength of 365 nm, shown in the inset of Fig. 4(a). Under irradiation of UV light with wavelengths of 340, 360 and 380 nm the emission remains at 450 nm. In Fig. 4(c), the absorption at 249 nm should be attributed to π→π* transmission of CC double bonds of conjugated species, which produced by violent reactions under hydrothermal treatment. Conjugation decreases the energy gap between π* and π molecular orbital, which causes red shift of the π→π* transmission and the decline of energy level of π* orbital. The peak at 340 nm should be due to n→π* transmission. The lone pair electrons come from hybrid atoms of oxygen or nitrogen of OX-CQDs. The red shift of n→π* transmission also occurs due to the drop of energy level of π* orbital. The distinct absorptions suggest the perfect luminescent emission.

The PL spectrum of OX-CQDs-C12H25 is similar to that of OX-CQDs, shown in Fig. 4(b). The emission of OX-CQDs-C12H25 remains unchanged. It claims that the quaternization with 1-chlorododecane do not alter the PL core of OX-CQDs and weaken the PL intensity obviously. That is significant for the fluorescent QY of the product. UV–vis spectra of OX-CQDs-C12H25 in Fig. 4(c) is likely with that of OX-CQDs, which discloses that the quaternization does not change the energy levels of molecular orbitals since the attached hydrocarbon chains do not participate the conjugation.

The peak areas of fluorescent spectra (340 nm excitation) varied with the absorbances are plotted in Fig. 4(d). The fluorescent QY of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 can therefore be obtained by comparing with the standard substance of quinine sulfate in 0.05 mol L−1 sulfuric acid, which are 23.13 and 16.21％ respectively. Such a fluorescent QY is far greater than that of CQDs prepared from dimethyloctadecyl-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl] ammonium chloride and glycerol [25].

Similarly, quaternization with 1-chlorododecane on CQDs do not alert the fluorescent emission (see Fig. S3(a)) but depresses the fluorescent QY. The values decrease from 16.08% to 12.47% (calculated by Fig. S3(b)). Fluorescent QY of CQDs-C12H25 is somewhat less than that of OX-CQDs. This fact suggests addition of hydrogen peroxide during preparation of the CQDs is significant for enhancement of the fluorescent QY.

OX-CQDs-C12H25 performs perfectly in reducing the surface tension of water, which revealed in Fig. 5(a). Surface tension of water drops rapidly by addition of OX-CQDs-C12H25. With the increment of the concentrations, the surface tension approaches a minimum of 30.00 mN m−1 approximately, which corresponds to the critical micelle concentration of 10 mg mL−1. Surface tension of pure water is dropped by 58.3%. That endows water enhanced capabilities of washing, bubbling and permeability. As to sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate, a typical surfactant, surface tension of the solution is 33.42 mN m−1 at the same concentration according to our measurements. That illustrates OX-CQDs-C12H25 is superior to sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate in this aspect.

Surface tension of water is also decreased tremendously by CQDs-C12H25, which exhibited in Fig. S3(c) of Supporting Information. The minimum reaches 34.87 mN m−1. The value is slightly greater than that of OX-CQDs-C12H25. This illustrates that raw material of hydrogen peroxide benefits the enhancement of surface activity of the surfactant. Oxidation of hydrogen peroxide is advantageous to production of oxygen-contained functional groups during the production of the CQDs that may tune the hydrophile-lipophile balance of OX-CQDs-C12H25.

Measurements of contact angle on OX-CQDs-C12H25 solutions also exhibit the performance of reducing surface tension of water, which exhibited in Fig. 6. The values of 70.1, 49.8 and 43.9° correspond to 2, 6 and 12 mg mL−1 respectively, which is descendent considerably compared with pure water of 77.4° in this case. This agrees with the measurement of surface tensions.

Table 1 shows that surface tensions of the mixed solutions decline with increased amounts of OX-CQDs-C12H25. The value reaches 29.94 mN m−1 when 0.3 g OX-CQDs-C12H25 are added, which is less than that of OX-CQDs-C12H25 or sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate solution obviously. That declares the synergistic interaction in reducing surface tension of water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretic analyses on surface absorption capacity of OX-CQDs-C12H25 solution
ce:section.ce:para:
Surface tension of the solution (γ) varied with the concentration (c) depicted in Fig. 5(a) can be fitted with a math function (see SI). Thus the surface absorption capacity can be calculated according to Gibb’s adsorption isotherm equation of
(1)
Γ = −
a
R T ⋅ n
⋅
d γ
d a
where Γ represents adsorption capacity (mol m−2); a denotes activity of a solution; T is absolute temperature (K); R is gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1); n is the number of ionic species; n = 2 is generally accepted [26,27]. If the solution is terrific dilute, the activity can be replaced with molar concentration approximately. In this case, Eq. (1) becomes
(2)
Γ = −
c
R T ⋅ n
⋅
d γ
d c

According to theory of solution absorption, surface absorption capacity (Γ) of a solution grows gradually with increase of the concentration until a saturated absorption capacity reaches. The value of dγ/dc in Eq. (2) is the derivatives of the fitted curves shown in Fig. 5(a). If saturated adsorption of the solution arrives Γ should reach a maximum. It satisfies the formula of
(3)
A =
1
L ⋅
Γ
m
where A represents sectional areas of a hydrophobic group; Γ m represents saturated adsorption capacity; L is Avogadro constant (6.02 × 1023 mol−1). The absorption capacities corresponding to the range of 0–2.0 mg mL−1 can be calculated via Eq. (2) as is plotted in Fig. 5(b). With increases of the concentration the values of Γ in Eq. (2) augment gradually until a maximum arrives (Γ m = 3.106 ×10−6 mol m2) [28–30]. Thus the sectional area of a hydrophobic group (A) is 0.535 nm2 [31] according to Eq. (3). As we know that the sectional area of a hydrocarbon chain such as an octadecanoic acid is 0.205 nm2. Accordingly, an OX-CQDs-C12H25 should attach with two more hydrocarbon chains averagely (0.535 nm2 / 0.205 nm2 = 2.608) that is appropriate for achieving a favorable hydrophile-lipophile-balance. This discloses that as-prepared OX-CQDs-C12H25 possesses multiple hydrophilic groups and multiple hydrophobic groups that should belong to Gemini surfactant.

Surface activity is the most vital parameter for a surfactant. Some Gemini cationic surfactant performed very well in this aspect. For example, such a surfactant prepared by esterification between ethylene glycol and chloroacetic acid firstly and by quaternization of N,N-dimethyldodecylamine successively declined surface tension of water from 71.0 to 36.8 mN m−1 [31]. A quaternary ammonium salts of 1,3–2(lauramide propyl dimethyl ammonium chloride) isopropyl alcohol reduced the surface tension of water to 32.27 mN m−1 [32]. Surface tension of the solution of a Gemini cationic surfactant derived from esterification of hydroquinone and chloroacetyl chloride and successive quaternization with higher tertiary amine reached 32.29 mN m−1 [33]. Similar product prepared with 1,3-dihydroxypropane also realized the decline of surface tension of water (the surface tension was 43.24 mN m−1) [34]. OX-CQDs-C12H25 described in this work is superior to these products in declining surface tension of water. Moreover, the approach reduces the manufacture cost dramatically since the hydrophilic groups of the cationic surfactant are derived from cheap carbohydrates. The technique herein is more convenient and efficient. Hydrothermal synthesis of OX-CQDs satisfies the demand of green chemical production. The synergistic effect with sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate which indicated in Table 1 exclaims that OX-CQDs-C12H25 can be a component of a compounded detergent with multiple functions. Luminescent OX-CQDs-C12H25 endows the capability of fluorescent whitening for the detergent. That performance is similar to organic fluorescent dye which is applied widely in industries of detergent and paper-making. However, the aromatic fluorescent dye may be harmful for human’s health. In this aspect, the novel cationic surfactant of OX-CQDs-C12H25 with benzene-free provides a more safety reagent for the fluorescent whitening.

The antibiosis of OX-CQDs and OX-CQDs-C12H25 against Escherichia coli were quantitatively evaluated, which illustrated in Fig. 7. Quaternizations on OX-CQDs enhance the antibiosis remarkably. OX-CQDs-C12H25 performs wonderful in prohibiting the growth of Escherichia coli. When the concentration of OX-CQDs-C12H25 is diluted at 0.21 mg mL−1 (this value is far less than the critical micelle concentration revealed in Fig. 5(a)), the antibacterial percentage still surpasses 95%. This is analogical with some conventional cationic surfactant [35–38]. The positively charged head groups interact with the negative charges on surface of cellular cytoplasmic membrane. Hence, the hydrophobic chains penetrate the membrane and disturb the selective permeability. That causes the death of the cell ultimately. CQDs-C12H25 also has the similar antibacterial performance, which displayed in Fig. S3(d) of SI. The effective concentration is 1.6 mg mL−1, which is far more than that of OX-CQDs-C12H25. It declares that antibiosis of OX-CQDs-C12H25 for Escherichia coli is superior to that of CQDs-C12H25.

Summarily, we provide a facile approach of preparing a novel surfactant derived from the CQDs with versatile performances. Preparation of the CQDs and the successive quaternization are implemented conveniently. The inherent oxygen-contained functional groups of the CQDs provide proper hydrophilicity while the attached alkyl chains by quaternization endow appropriate hydrophobicity. Addition of hydrogen peroxide during preparing the CQDs favors the cationic surfactant with more powerful surface activity and antibiotic activity. The versatility of surface activity, fluorescence and antibiosis of the cationic CQDs surfactant predicts extensive applications.
Yuan Li: Writing, Synthesis. Peng Wu: Measurements of antibacterial activity. Chen Lei: Characterization. Xiang Liu: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xinya Han: Reviewing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0118200); Anhui Natural Science Fund Project (No. 1908085MB35) and Anhui Key Research and Development Program (No. 201904b11020025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S. Yu, J. Wei, H. Xiong, Full-Color Light-Emitting Carbon Dots with a Surface-State-Controlled Luminescence Mechanism, ACS Nano 10 (2016) 484‐491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yang, J. Cui, M. Zheng, C. Hu, S. Tan, Y. Xiao, Q. Yang, Y. Liu, One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan, Chem. Commun. 48 (2011) 380‐382.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces
